caption: Figure 1: Left, a hypermap that admits the boundary conditions ◦•◦•••◦•••◦◦◦ and ◦•◦••◦◦•••◦•◦. Right, a hypermap with a monochromatic boundary.
description2: A hypermap is plotted, featuring two structures: one admitting specific boundary conditions and another with a monochromatic boundary on the right side.
description3: Two-panel image displays a hypermap with boundary conditions on the left, and a monochromatic boundary hypermap on the right.
questions: What are the differences between the boundary conditions shown on the left and right?
How do the hypermaps presented differ in their boundary characteristics?

caption: Figure 2: Left, a hypermap with an alternating boundary. Right, a hypermap whose boundary is not alternating.
description2: The figure displays two structures, a hypermap with an alternating boundary on the left and one without an alternating boundary on the right.
description3: Two-panel plot displays a hypermap with an alternating boundary on the left and a hypermap with a non-alternating boundary on the right.
questions: What is the difference between the hypermaps shown on the left and right?
How do the boundaries of the hypermaps differ between the two panels?

caption: Figure 3: Illustration of the many-to-one correspondence, from hypermaps to Ising-decorated maps, through the contraction of digons.
description2: The illustration depicts a many-to-one correspondence between hypermaps and Ising-decorated maps achieved by contracting digons.
description3: Two-panel diagram displays a transformation, showing a complex shape evolving into a simpler, grid-like structure through a series of arrows.
questions: What is the relationship between hypermaps and Ising-decorated maps?
How does the contraction of digons transform a hypermap into a map?

caption: Figure 4: A sketch of the splitting procedure: top left, the boundary edge of type A that we peel. Top right, the case where it is adjacent to a black inner face. Bottom row, the case where it is identified with a boundary edge of type B.
description2: A sketch illustrates the splitting procedure, featuring peeling a boundary edge of type A and its adjacency to a black inner face or identification with a boundary edge of type B.
description3: Two-panel sketch illustrates boundary edge peeling procedures, showing type A edges and their adjacency to a black inner face, and type B edges.
questions: What are the different scenarios shown regarding the boundary edge splitting procedure?
How does the splitting procedure change when the boundary edge is adjacent to a black face?
